skill.name: azure-communication-callautomation-java
skill.description: Build Java call automation workflows with Azure Communication Services, including IVR, routing, recording, DTMF, speech recognition, and text-to-speech examples.
skill.summary: Implement Azure Communication Services Call Automation workflows in Java.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-crypto alerts are false positives caused by Markdown fences, inline code, and ordinary Java examples. The skill is a documentation-oriented ACS Call Automation guide, but it handles telephony workflows with callbacks, recordings, connection strings, and DTMF data, so publication should include a warning about sensitive data handling and webhook validation.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive DTMF Data Logging Example
medium_findings.description: The DTMF example asks for an account number and later prints collected tones. This could expose customer account data in application logs if copied directly into production code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The guide explicitly prompts for an account number and prints DTMF tones. This is a clear sensitive-data exposure risk, although it appears to be an educational example rather than malicious code.

medium_findings.title: Webhook Authentication Not Shown
medium_findings.description: The callback handler parses requestBody directly without showing callback signature validation, origin checks, or replay protection. Implementers could expose call control logic to forged webhook requests.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sample handler demonstrates parsing webhook events but does not include validation. The omission is risky for call automation systems, but the short guide may rely on implementers to add production controls.

medium_findings.title: Call Recording and Local Download Handling
medium_findings.description: The recording example starts audio-video recording and downloads the recording to a local file path. Recordings can contain regulated personal data and require consent, retention, access control, and secure storage.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly demonstrates call recording and local file download. This is legitimate ACS functionality, but the data sensitivity creates a real operational security concern.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many backtick locations as Ruby or shell execution. These are Markdown code fences and inline code spans containing Java, XML, tables, and bash-style environment examples, not executable skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows fenced documentation examples, not Ruby backtick execution. No instruction causes the assistant to execute these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Network URLs Are Legitimate Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to Azure Communication Services endpoints, callback placeholders, and an Azure Blob Storage audio example. They document expected ACS integration points and do not show data exfiltration intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are placeholders or Azure service URLs used by the documented SDK. No suspicious third-party collector or credential transmission pattern is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The flagged frontmatter description does not contain a weak cryptographic algorithm or crypto operation. No evidence found for MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar insecure cryptographic use in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the flagged line shows a natural-language skill description only. The reviewed skill file contains no cryptographic implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Connection String Placeholder in Environment Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide shows an ACS connection string environment variable containing an access key placeholder. Users should store this secret in a managed secret store and avoid committing real values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The value is a placeholder, so no secret is exposed. The pattern is still security-relevant because copied examples can lead to secret leakage in repositories or logs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Console Logging of Call Metadata
dangerous_patterns.description: The webhook example prints call identifiers, DTMF tones, and error messages. Production systems should redact sensitive identifiers and avoid logging caller-provided input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples contain direct System.out.println calls for call events and input-derived values. The risk depends on production logging practices, but the sensitive context is clear.

content.user_title: Build Azure Call Automation in Java
content.value_statement: Teams need reliable examples for IVR, call routing, recording, and recognition with Azure Communication Services. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Java SDK patterns for call automation workflows.
actual_capabilities: Shows Maven dependency setup for the Azure Communication Services Call Automation Java SDK.
Demonstrates client creation with DefaultAzureCredential and connection string options.
Provides examples for outbound calls, incoming call answering, transfers, and hang-up actions.
Explains media operations, including text-to-speech, audio file playback, DTMF, and speech recognition.
Includes call recording, recording download, webhook event parsing, and basic error handling examples.
limitations: The skill is a guide and does not provide a runnable application scaffold.
Webhook authentication, replay protection, and production security controls are not fully implemented.
Examples use placeholders for endpoints, phone numbers, user IDs, and credentials.
Compliance requirements for recording, consent, retention, and regional telephony rules are not covered.

use_cases.title: Build an IVR Prototype
use_cases.description: Create a Java proof of concept that answers calls, plays prompts, collects DTMF input, and routes callers.

use_cases.title: Add Call Recording Workflows
use_cases.description: Implement recording start, pause, resume, stop, and download flows for controlled service interactions.

use_cases.title: Review ACS Event Handling
use_cases.description: Map incoming webhook events to application actions for connected, recognized, playback, and disconnected call states.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Client
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an ACS Call Automation Java project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-communication-callautomation-java skill to show the Maven dependency and a secure CallAutomationClient setup with DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Design an IVR Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate call flow with prompts and DTMF collection.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a Java IVR flow that answers an incoming call, plays a menu, collects DTMF input, and routes the caller.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Webhook Events
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate backend integration for ACS callback events.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline Java webhook handling for CallConnected, RecognizeCompleted, PlayCompleted, and CallDisconnected events, including validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Production Call Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan a production ACS call automation service with recording, speech recognition, secret handling, consent, logging controls, and failure handling.

output_examples.input: Create a Java IVR flow for sales and support routing.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step implementation plan covering client setup, incoming call answering, text-to-speech prompts, DTMF recognition, routing decisions, and callback event handling.

output_examples.input: Explain how to add call recording to an ACS call automation service.
output_examples.output: A workflow summary for starting, pausing, resuming, stopping, downloading, and securing recordings, with notes about consent and retention controls.

output_examples.input: Review my ACS webhook handler design.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering event parsing, callback validation, replay prevention, redacted logging, error handling, and mapping events to call actions.

best_practices: Use managed identity or DefaultAzureCredential when possible, and keep connection strings in a secret store.
Validate callback authenticity and redact call identifiers, DTMF input, and speech results before logging.
Add explicit consent, retention, access control, and encryption policies before enabling recording.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real ACS connection strings, access keys, phone numbers, or callback secrets into source files.
Do not log account numbers, DTMF tones, speech transcripts, or full exception messages in production.
Do not expose webhook endpoints without authentication, replay protection, and rate limiting.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands or deploy resources?
faq.answer: No. It provides Java SDK guidance and examples. You still need to build, test, and deploy your application.

faq.question: Can I use this with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code for implementation guidance.

faq.question: Does it include a complete production IVR application?
faq.answer: No. It gives focused examples for common ACS Call Automation operations, not a full application template.

faq.question: How should I handle ACS credentials?
faq.answer: Prefer managed identity or DefaultAzureCredential. Store any connection strings in a secret manager, not in code.

faq.question: Are recording compliance rules included?
faq.answer: No. You must add consent, disclosure, retention, access control, and regional compliance checks for your use case.

faq.question: What should I add before using the webhook sample?
faq.answer: Add callback validation, replay protection, request size limits, rate limiting, and redacted logging before production use.
